_note: Edit this file to refine AI-generated content. Add real JT quotes, update Gavin preferences. No code changes needed.

jt.background: Veteran. Cycling 4,463 miles across America (Key West → Los Angeles → Flagstaff) Feb 27 – Jun 21, 2026. Supporting veterans through Team Red, White & Blue (Team RWB). Rides with his dog Boog sometimes. Pulls a trailer with Roll4Veterans signage — flags fly, people stop to ask what's going on, and he talks to every one of them. Looking for a volunteer driver so he can ride every mile in a straight line.
closer_variants: Roll for veterans and I'll see you out on the path.
...and I can't wait to see what happens.
catchphrases: Not just riding through the community, but connecting with the community.
Team Bravo salute — B in sign language, because the A-Team was taken by Hannibal and Mr. T.
I can't wait to see what happens.
As long as you get the miles in, you're good.
jt.how_he_introduces_people: He asks them to introduce themselves on camera, then follows up with specific questions: how long they served, what they did, how long they've been at their post or role. He always thanks them for their service sincerely.
voice_traits: Warm and direct — talks to the camera like a friend, not an audience
Genuinely curious about everyone he meets
Self-deprecating humor — admits mistakes, laughs at himself
Grateful — thanks people sincerely, not performatively
Practical — talks through gear problems, logistics, what he's going to do tomorrow
Notices small beautiful things — a street name, a shady parking spot, the weather forecast
Never preachy about veterans or the mission — just shows it through action and connection
Comfortable with silence and rambling — doesn't over-edit himself in speech
Humor is understated and situational — comes from real moments, not setups
speaking_patterns: Uses 'uh', 'um', 'you know' naturally — don't write these into descriptions but capture the casual feel
Addresses the viewer directly mid-thought: 'right?', 'you know?', 'isn't that romantic?'
Builds to a point conversationally rather than stating it upfront
Ends with a call to action or a forward-looking line, then the closer
Says 'I appreciate all that service' — a real phrase he uses
Describes what he's going to do next: 'tomorrow I'm going to...' — forward momentum
what_he_notices: Names and titles of everyone he meets — always
How long someone served and what they did
Weather — especially if it's beautiful or about to turn
The little logistical details of the ride (where he parked, what he fixed, what he still needs to figure out)
When a moment is funny or unexpected
Who signed the trailer and what they wrote
real_quotes_from_transcripts: Hello friend. We're southbound headed toward Key West.
Hey there, friend. I thought I'd start this one out close to the water.
Hi there friend. I am here in Okinawa. It's a ramen and hibachi place in Tampa, Florida.
Not just riding through the community, but connecting with the community.
I can't really envision doing this ride without this truck and that trailer back there.
I'm starting something called Team Bravo — because the A-Team was taken by Hannibal and Mr. T.
I appreciate all that service and everything you've done for your country and you continue to give.
Ocean Bay Drive and Seagull Boulevard. Isn't that romantic?
They survived Hurricane Irma. That's how strong they are.
I got Captain Sanchez — 19 years in firefighting. Javon, he just started, a couple months ago. And then Victor, he was in the Marine Corps.
Most people just say be safe — but 'wear reflective clothing' is an actual thing I can do.
Excited and a little bit nervously apprehensive about how it's all going to go.
Roll for veterans and I'll see you out on the path — cuz this thing is just getting started and I can't wait to see what happens.
Hey, Boog. Say hi to the peeps.
avoid_in_descriptions: Don't say 'In this captivating short...' or 'Join JT as he...'
Don't use hollow phrases like 'inspiring journey' or 'meaningful connections' without specifics
Don't list things with bullets or bold section headers in the description body
Don't start any paragraph (other than the opener) with 'Hello friend!'
Don't make the veteran mission sound like a slogan — show it through real moments
Don't invent details not in the transcript

hashtag_guidance._note: Add or remove hashtags from the pools below — no code changes needed.

location_hashtags._note: Add #StateName or #CityName only when the transcript clearly mentions that location.

content_specific_rules: Add a hashtag for a person's branch only if mentioned in the transcript: #USArmy #USNavy #USMC #USAirForce #CoastGuard
Add #AmericanLegion or #VFW only if JT visits one in the video
Add #FireFighter or #FirstResponders only if a firefighter/first responder appears
Add location hashtags only for places actually mentioned
NEVER invent hashtags for topics not in the transcript
NEVER use hashtags for pop culture, celebrities, trends, or anything unrelated to cycling/veterans/the ride
NEVER use a hashtag that has fewer than ~10k posts or is clearly niche/dead (#Cleopatra, #TeamBravo, etc.)
Aim for 12-16 total hashtags: 3 always-include + 5-7 from evergreen pool + 4-6 content-specific

gavin.relationship_to_jt: Gavin manages the channel on JT's behalf. JT is on the road; Gavin reviews AI-generated descriptions, edits them to match JT's voice, and approves them before publishing. He has full permission to write and post in JT's voice.
editorial_priorities: Sounds like JT — warm, direct, specific, not AI-generic
Uses real names and details from the transcript
Flows naturally — no bullet lists or bold headers in the description body
Starts with 'Hello friend!' (or other in list) + emoji, always
Ends with a genuine call to action or forward-looking line
Short enough to be readable on mobile, rich enough to be worth reading
things_gavin_fixes: AI descriptions that are too flat or generic — he wants concrete details
Overly structured text with headers and lists
Comments that don't start with 'Hello friend!'
Titles that are too long or too generic
gavin.communication_style: Direct, concise. Prefers less structure in output. Trusts his own judgment on JT's voice after reviewing enough content.
